title: Rise of the Machines: The Impact of Automated Underwriting
abstract: Using a randomized experiment in auto lending, we find that algorithmic underwriting outperforms the human underwriting process, resulting in 10.2% higher loan profits and 6.8% lower default rates. The human and machine underwriters show similar performance for low-risk, less complex loans. However, the performance of human underwritten loans largely declines for riskier and more complex loans, whereas the machine performance stays relatively stable across various risk dimensions and loan characteristics. The performance difference is more pronounced at underwriting thresholds with a high potential for agency conflict. These results are consistent with algorithmic underwriting mitigating agency conflicts and humans’ limited capacity for analyzing complex problems.

author.Mark Jansen: Department of Finance, University of Utah, Salt Lake City, Utah 84103
author.Hieu Quang Nguyen: Department of Applied Finance, Macquarie University, Sydney, New South Wales 2109, Australia
author.Amin Shams: Department of Finance, Fisher College of Business, The Ohio State University, Columbus, Ohio 43210

article.1. Introduction: Rapidly developing technology has transformed the consumer lending market over the last few decades, but the effects of these changes are widely debated. Lenders increasingly automate underwriting to speed credit decisions and save on labor costs, but thecompleteautomation of underwriting may lead to the loss of human expertise that can complement algorithms in complex situations (Berg 2015,Brogaard et al. 2020,Costello et al. 2020). Human underwriters rely on their discretion to assess nuanced information, especially when they evaluate borrowers with lower credit quality (Iyer et al. 2016). The extant literature in this area discusses the impacts of FinTech lending and improved machine learning algorithms (Fuster et al. 2020) and considers the machine-related changes in customer selection (Di Maggio and Yao 2020,Sutherland 2020). However, the direct effect of replacing decision making by humans with decision making by machines remains unclear. In this paper, we study the impact of fully replacing human underwriters with machines in consumer lending markets and examine mechanisms that give rise to the differential outcomes in this setting. We use a randomized experiment in the auto finance industry to examine and quantify the differences in the terms and outcomes of loans underwritten by two systems: traditional human underwriting and full automation. We examine differences in loan profitability, pricing, and default risk and then compare the outcomes across various loan characteristics. Additionally, we examine potential underlying mechanisms for the outcomes we observe. The experiment we use involved a major indirect auto lender that started automating its underwriting process in 2013. Auto loans are a substantial liability on households’ balance sheets. Nearly half (48%) of households that have debt hold vehicle-related debt (Bhutta et al. 2020). The total amount of such debt is large and increasing: Vehicle loan balances in the United States exceeded $1.3 trillion in 2021 (Zabritski 2021). By way of comparison, securitizations of auto loans in 2021 were three times larger than the combined securitizations of credit cards and student loans (source: Bloomberg, Dealogic, Thomson Reuters). About 80% of vehicle-related debt arises from the indirect lending channel, which we investigate in this study (Low et al. 2020). In indirect auto lending, dealerships send a consumer’s loan application to several lenders. An underwriter at each lender evaluates the risk of the borrower and either rejects the application or submits a bid on the interest rate (APR) in an auction with other participating lenders. The dealer then chooses the bid with the lowest rate. During the experiment, our data provider randomly assigned half of its loan applications to human underwriters and the other half to a machine that set the bid algorithmically. Importantly, all the loans were drawn from the same pool of applications, and both the machine algorithm and the human underwriting guidelines relied on the same historical loan data. This resulted in a rich sample of approximately 140,000 auto loans from more than 4,000 U.S. car dealerships, underwritten either by machine or human. This setting is particularly informative about the outcomes of lenders’ and insurance companies’ shift from human and machine-aided human processes to full automation (Johansson and Vogelgesang 2016,Chappell et al. 2018). The absence of the confounding effect of soft information allows us to isolate the impact of a fully automated underwriting system relative to a human system that has been in place for decades. Consequently, our study yields an important performance benchmark for studies assessing the incremental value of soft information and human-aided machine systems. We calculate loan profitability using realized cash flows and find that machine-underwritten loans are 10.2% more profitable than human-underwritten loans originated in the same month.1This is equal to a 2.7-percentage-point increase relative to the baseline profit of 26.4% for human-underwritten loans. Themedianprofitability of machine-underwritten loans is comparable to the profitability at the 92nd percentile of the human underwriters. Moreover, while the human underwriters are more likely to fund a loan application (1.5% higher funding rates), the difference in quantity does not make up for the higher profitability of the machine-underwritten loans; the aggregate dollar profit of the machine-underwritten portfolio is 9.9% higher than that of the human-underwritten portfolio. All these results are robust to using different specifications and controls. When we examine the source of this profit differential, we find that loan pricing explains part of the result. Underwriters set the APR based on the perceived risk profile of the borrower using both an underwriting guideline and their discretion. We find that human-underwritten loans have a significantly lower APR (by 44.2 basis points) than machine-underwritten loans that were originated in the same month. The difference in pricing holds even after we control for loan and borrower characteristics; vehicle make and model; and dealership fixed effects. This finding suggests that human underwriters offer lower prices on loans that are ex ante similar. Moreover, consistent with human underwriters using their discretion or taking into account nonstandard information they obtain from the dealers, human-underwritten loans show large variations in APR that cannot be explained by observable risk indicators. Although we control for ex ante risk characteristics, the higher APR of machine-underwritten loans would seem to suggest that the algorithm selects loans that are more likely to default ex post. However, we find that the realized default rate over the full term of the loans is 6.8%lowerfor machine-underwritten loans. Moreover, machine-underwritten loans have 6.9% and 8.3% lower early default rates in the first 24 and 36 months of their lives, respectively, relative to human-underwritten loans. In short, the machine-underwritten loans not only have a higher interest rate but are also less risky. Having quantified the performance improvement in the automated system, we examine potential explanations for why machine-underwritten loans outperform human-underwritten loans. There are several differences between the human- and machine-based underwriting systems, but our primary focus is on two novel explanations. The first centers on agency conflicts. Human underwriters, unlike machines, have incentives to maximize their chance of winning the loan auction by underpricing the loan. If agency conflict affects loan performance, we expect to see a more pronounced difference in profitability when the potential for conflicts of interest is highest. The second focuses on machines’ and humans’ different capacities for analyzing complex data. Human underwriters may be less capable of analyzing the complexities in loan applications to accurately price the risk. If this is the case, we expect the difference in profitability to increase with the complexity of predicting the loan outcome. The common practice in the industry is that human underwriters evaluate loan applications using a set of guidelines based on cutoffs of risk factors (e.g., credit score, loan-to-value (LTV) ratio, and debt-to-income (DTI) ratio). Lenders devise these guidelines to compensate for human limitations in information processing (Kahneman 1973,Chui et al. 2016). Such simplifications may lead to gaming by underwriters, especially around the key cutoffs (Keys et al. 2010,Griffin and Maturana 2016). In contrast, the machine fits predictive models to a continuous variable space, using the same historical data as in the human underwriter guidelines. By comparing human and machine decisions around these key cutoffs, we can examine whether, and to what extent, machines can mitigate the agency issues that arise in underwriting. Many auto lenders, including our data provider, use the LTV cutoff of 125% as a threshold for categorizing loan applications into different risk tiers. Loans with an LTV higher than 125% fall into a high-risk tier and are therefore priced higher and subject to greater restrictions on other risk measures (e.g., debt-to-income ratio). A possibly unintended effect of these guidelines is that they give underwriters leeway to fund loans that fall just below the threshold (and which are still fairly risky) at more competitive risk-adjusted rates. Underwriters who do so can maximize their chance of winning bids and of increasing their monthly commissions.2 Consistent with this intuition, we observe that human underwriters originate a cluster of risky yet underpriced loans right below the LTV cutoff of 125%. Immediately below the cutoff, we observe that human underwriters are 6.4 times more likely to fund loans, relative to the machine (a pattern driven by a higher approval rate by human underwriters); human-underwritten loans possess significantly riskier observable characteristics than machine-underwritten loans; and human-underwritten loans have a 38.1% higher realized default rate in the first 36 months, relative to machine-underwritten loans. Because the human-underwritten loans lack a higher APR to compensate for their greater risk, the machine-underwritten loans just below the cutoff are 27.9% more profitable. These findings suggest that incentives for meeting loan production targets could at least partially explain the underperformance of human-underwritten loans relative to machine-underwritten loans.3 Next, we examine the impact of loan complexity on performance. Our prediction in this area is not clear ex ante. On the one hand, the machine has a superior capacity to handle complex data. On the other hand, the complete automation of underwriting results in the loss of human discretion. Human discretion could aid algorithms in complex situations (Berg 2015,Brogaard et al. 2020,Costello et al. 2020), such as when the model’s underlying data are thin (Finlay 2008,Iyer et al. 2016) or when the borrowers are low income and therefore experience larger income volatility (Dynan et al. 2012). We quantify loan complexity as the expected magnitude of the forecast errors for predicting default. This magnitude captures the difficulty in predicting loan outcomes accurately. We use out-of-sample estimates of the forecast errors conditional on loan, borrower, and vehicle characteristics from more than 15 years of historical loan data before the beginning of the experiment. We find a wide variation in the forecast errors conditional on various loan characteristics even after controlling for the risk of default.4 Using this measure, we find that the out-of-sample difference in loan profit between machine- and human-underwritten loans increases by one percentage point for a one-standard-deviation increase in complexity. The results hold even after controlling for default likelihood. Importantly, whereas the profitability of machine-underwritten loans is relatively stable across different deciles of complexity, the profitability of human-underwritten loans monotonically declines with loan complexity. Loans in the top two complexity deciles account for 59% of the dollar profit difference between the machine- and human-underwritten loans. We observe this result even though the human underwriters in our setting are privy to nonstandard information that might be especially helpful in complex cases. Overall, our findings suggest that the underperformance of the human underwriters is most pronounced in complex cases and when the potential for agency conflicts is high. This paper contributes to the nascent literature on the impact of technology adoption and alternative data in the lending industry (Goldstein et al. 2019). A broad literature on the automation of tasks alongside the implementation of machine algorithms has provided numerous valuable insights. (SeeBerg et al. (2021)for a comprehensive review of the literature.) For example, FinTech lenders have been shown to improve mortgage application processing times (Fuster et al. 2019) and reduce discrimination (Bartlett et al. 2019,D’Acunto et al. 2020). Lenders increasingly use automated underwriting and unconventional data to complement or even substitute for traditional credit bureau scores (Agarwal et al. 2019,Ravina 2019,Berg et al. 2020). And in marketplace lending, sophisticated investors who automatically screen and invest have been shown to outperform unsophisticated investors (Vallée and Zeng 2019).5 A first-order question in this literature is how the adoption of technology affects lender performance. Although the impact of machine algorithms specifically, and of FinTechs more generally, has received much attention, many gaps remain in our understanding of how replacing humans with algorithms plays out. Studies in this area are complicated by the fact that firms that adopt financial technologies are likely to differ greatly from legacy firms in dimensions beyond the technologies themselves. Our experimental setting allows us to quantify the direct impact of a widely adopted technology without having to compare FinTech and non-FinTech firms. In addition, the borrowers in our setting do not self-select. Our data set consists of loan applications, with similar borrower characteristics, which were received by the same lender but underwritten using two different underwriting systems (both of which are commonly used by lenders). In an apples-to-apples setting, we directly compare the traditional lending by human underwriters with the recently trending automated underwriting. This differs fromBerg (2015), who examines a setting in which a human incrementally improves on an automated scoring system; fromFuster et al. (2020), who examine algorithms of varying levels of sophistication; and fromFuster et al. (2019), who compare FinTech and non-FinTech firms. We also contribute to the literature on agency conflicts in the lending market (Udell 1989,Hertzberg et al. 2010,Heider and Inderst 2012,Berg 2015,Agarwal and Ben-David 2018) and, more specifically, on the potential gaming of discrete cutoffs in loan origination (Keys et al. 2010,Griffin and Maturana 2016). We find that loans perform poorly just below a critical LTV cutoff, but only when they are underwritten by humans. The results suggest that machine underwriting can mitigate agency issues in the lending industry. Moreover, the machine’s lower default rate is consistent with the machine being adept at reducing information asymmetry and the related adverse selection problem (Adams et al. 2009;Einav et al. 2012,2013;Dobbie et al. 2021). Overall, our experimental setting provides clear intuitions that can be generalized to a variety of financial underwriting settings where financial institutions face agency issues. (For example, mortgages and business loans also use discrete cutoffs for LTV.) The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. InSection 2, we provide a brief overview of indirect auto financing and describe the implementation of machine underwriting. InSection 3, we describe our data.Section 4presents the main results. InSection 5, we investigate the channels that explain the machine’s outperformance.Section 6concludes.
article.2. Overview of the Auto Financing and th: 2.1. Indirect Auto Financing We first examine the underwriting process in indirect auto financing. This process differs from direct financing, where buyers finance their auto purchase directly through a financial institution such as a credit union. In indirect financing, buyers instead finance their auto purchase with the dealership, which then sells the loan to a finance company. Indirect auto financing involves several steps. First, conditioned on the buyer expressing an interest in financing the vehicle purchase, dealers submit the customer’s credit application to multiple lenders through a standardized information platform such asDealer TrackorRoute One.6The lenders receiving the application use information from outside data providers (e.g., credit bureaus) to ascertain the riskiness of the borrower. Based on that information, the lender either denies the application or responds favorably with terms under which it will acquire the prospective loan from the dealership. Dealerships choose the loan offer that allows them to generate the highest profit, which is a multidimensional problem. First, dealerships determine if the borrower will qualify under the terms outlined in the lender offer. For example, a lender’s payment-to-income (PTI) guidelines are typically based on the loan term and the borrower’s employment history and credit score. If the borrower meets the terms of multiple lenders, the dealer can compare lender offers. The profit is a function of the advance rate (LTV), the loan discount rate, and dealership participation. Therefore, APR is just one element. The advance rate reflects the markup that lenders enjoy relative to the wholesale price of the vehicle. The numerator of LTV reflects the selling price of the vehicle and add-ons net of the borrower downpayment. Holding fixed borrower risk, lenders that allow higher LTVs are, in effect, permitting the dealership to charge a higher markup on the vehicle.7 The lenders’ auto financing guidelines also reflect a discount schedule which is correlated with borrower default risk. Similar to a junk bond, an auto loan of lower credit quality will be discounted more; that is, the lender will purchase it at a discount to par. These discount schedules vary by lender and typically equal 2%–12% of the wholesale value of the vehicle. Finally, dealerships enjoy reserve payments, which are factors that permit the loan to trade at a premium to par. These factors include (a) how much the dealership marked up the APR over the buy-rate (i.e., the offer rate from the lender) and (b) profit sharing from Guaranteed Asset Protection (GAP) insurance and credit disability insurance. Although the majority of subprime loans do not feature reserve payments, these payments can offset the discount, as they range from 3% to 11%. Having finalized the terms with the lender, the dealer then completes the sale of the vehicle with the borrower.8Over the next several days, the lender verifies the information on the borrower’s credit application. If the screening uncovers no problems, the lender acquires the loan and pays the dealer. If the verification process fails to confirm the details in the loan application, the loan is sold to the lender at a discount reflecting its higher risk.9 After acquiring the loan, the lender collects monthly payments from the borrower and retains the property right to the vehicle until the loan has been paid in full. If the borrower defaults, the lender attempts to repossess the vehicle and sell it at a public auction. If the vehicle sells for more than the outstanding loan amount net the recovery cost, then the borrower receives the balance. If there is an outstanding loan amount even after the sale of the repossessed vehicle, then the lender can sue the borrower for the balance. A lender that wins a judgment against a borrower attempts to collect additional funds, sometimes by garnishing the borrower’s wage (Brown and Jansen 2019). In the subprime auto loan market, defaults are frequent (average default rate is 23.6% in our sample), and postdefault recovery activity is common. More than 65,000 financial institutions across the United States finance auto loans. The market is competitive, with no single firm holding more than a 6% market share (Baines and Courchane 2014). Our data provider, which has been active for decades, is one of the top 10 nonprime lenders by market share. The company buys loans from thousands of dealerships in more than 40 states (Figure A.1 in the Online Appendix) and securitizes these loans after they have been seasoned for at least nine months.10 2.2. Underwriting Process The underwriters work for the lender and transact directly with the dealers. Minutes after reviewing a loan application, the underwriter either rejects it or submits a bid for it. The underwriters use lender guidelines to tier prospective borrowers based on loan risk. Figures A.2, A.3, and A.4 in the Online Appendix show examples of the guidelines used by two large lenders. In tiering the borrowers, the underwriters consider a borrower’s credit score, debt, income, employment (status, type, and tenure), time at current residence, and any past bankruptcy. They also consider the type, make, model, and mileage of the car, whether the car is used or new, and the vehicle’s wholesale value. As shown in Figure A.5 in the Online Appendix, the guidelines simplify the lending process for the underwriter. First, the underwriter decides if the loan qualifies—that is, whether the borrower characteristics are above the cutoff in the firm guidelines. Any loan above the cutoff is accepted and priced. Figure A.6 in the Online Appendix illustrates how a mapping of borrower risk factors into tiers affects loan pricing. Similar-risk borrowers in the same tier are offered similar interest rates. If the loan falls below the cutoff and does not qualify for funding, it is denied. As shown in Figure A.6 in the Online Appendix, borrowers who are slightly riskier than the threshold of the tier either pay higher prices or are denied credit. In marginal cases, however, dealers may give the human underwriter additional information to help strengthen the borrower’s application. Dealers can push for a better tier (i.e., a lower interest rate) by including, in the application’s comment section, information that goes beyond the standard borrower credit attributes. The comment section, which is occupied in 18.3% of loans, typically notes recent changes in the borrower’s creditworthiness (e.g., updates on employment or corrections to information in the credit profile) that are not reflected in the credit bureau tradelines.11Consistent with the findings ofAdelino et al. (2019), we find, in unreported results, that the comment section is uncorrelated with loan pricing or loan outcomes, suggesting that human underwriters give it little consideration when they make the credit decision and price the loan. This absence of credible soft information allows us to evaluate the performance differences between machine and human underwriters in a setting where both underwriter types rely on the same historical data to guide their decisions.12 2.3. Implementation of Machine Underwriting In 2013, the data provider launched an automated underwriting system capable of fully replacing human underwriters in lending decisions. To evaluate the effectiveness of the automated underwriting process, the data provider set up a randomized experiment. For the next four years, each credit application the firm received would be randomly assigned to either the machine underwriter or a human underwriter (with probability 50%). One limitation of our data are that we only observe loans that are funded, not the full set of applications.13To affirm (ex post) that the randomization was successful, we compare the number of loans originated (1) per day of the month and (2) for each day of the week. In unreported results, we find that the daily percentage of loans underwritten by the machine and human is close to 50-50 (except on Sundays, when few humans work). In robustness tests, we find that including day of the month and day of the week fixed effects does not change our results, as shown in Table A.3 in the Online Appendix. We continue our examination of the randomization process by looking at the exact timing of the assignment of loan applications to each underwriting system. Using application timestamps, we can determine the exact times (down to the second) the lender received applications for funded loans. We find the application time, which is not controlled by the lender, to be random down to the second (no significant difference between machine and human with anFstatistic of 2.23). The randomness of the application time helps to support the claim that the assignment of the applications is randomized.14 In cases when the application was assigned to the machine, the machine responded to the auction without human intervention, assessing the borrower’s risk relative to historical loan performance. The machine was trained through supervised learning using historical data on whether a loan became delinquent within the first 24 months.15The model was used to predict the default likelihood of new applicants, which was then used to price the loans. We use data from this randomized experiment to compare the performance of loans underwritten by the machine and humans. We observe loan and borrower characteristics along with a unique ID for each underwriter, be they human or machine. One limitation of the study is that we cannot observe the characteristics of the rejected loans. However, the number of machine-underwritten loans is roughly the same as the number of human-underwritten loans, suggesting that the machine accepts and wins the bidding as frequently as the human underwriters.16
article.3. Data: Our novel data set includes all loans that the lender acquired in 40 states during the experiment. In total, we observe characteristics of approximately 140,000 loans acquired from more than 4,000 U.S. dealerships located in more than 1,900 U.S. ZIP codes. These loans are originated either by the machine or by one of the 153 human underwriters working for the lender during our sample period. For each of these loans, we observe all loan origination information received by the lender, as well as monthly performance data through July 2019. The data include characteristics of the borrower (e.g., income, credit score, homeownership, bankruptcy history), the vehicle (e.g., make, model, book value, age, mileage), and the loan (e.g., price, LTV, term, loan amount, default).17The main results compare the performance of machine and human underwriters, starting with the experiment’s inception in 2013. Our analysis also uses loans originated prior to 2013 to build a credit model that predicts the performance of the ongoing loans during the experiment. As auto loans commonly have terms in excess of five years, the full sample includes both completed loans and loans with censored outcomes. In our sample, 58.7% of the loans were completed before the end of the sample period. We observe the loan outcome for the completed loans: paid in full as scheduled, prepaid, or end in default. In the case of a prepayment, the exact timing schedule of the prepaid cash flows are not reported in the data. Table A.1 in the Online Appendix reports that the difference in the prepayment rates between human- and machine-underwritten loans is not statistically significant.18In the case of a default, we observe how much the lender recovers from the sale of the vehicle and postdefault collection efforts. For ongoing loans, we observe the payments up to the final date of the sample period. In addition to the results for the full sample, we report results for subsamples of completed loans, as well as for loans whose original terms extend beyond (censored) or do not extend beyond (uncensored) the end of our sample period. 3.1. Measuring Loan Profitability Our data allow us to assess loan origination terms and performance using traditional measures such as the interest rate (APR) and the default rate. However, because the default rate of subprime auto loans is high—more than 26% during our sample period—subprime lenders may perceive defaults not as a failure of screening but as an unavoidable part of the business. By putting policies in place to recover loan deficiencies in case of default (e.g., repossessing the cars, garnishing wages, etc.) and by charging high interest rates that generate significant cash flow early in the loan term, lenders can still profit when some loans default. To account for this feature of the market, our study considers not only the default rate and the APR but also the profitability of the loans based on the present value of the cash flows received from the borrowers. This profit measure takes into account the discount from the face value enjoyed by the lender, as well as the principal loss after recovery in case of default. For a fully paid-off loan, the profit is calculated as the sum of present values of monthly payments minus the initial investment.19For a loan that defaults, the profit is the sum of the present values of monthly payments up to the point of default plus the discounted value of the recovered losses minus the initial investment, as shown inEquation (1). We then calculate the percentage profit as the profit scaled by the initial investment, as shown inEquation (2). In our sample, 58.7% of the loans were completed before the end of our sample period; these loans either defaulted or were fully paid by July 2019. We use the realized cash flows to calculate the profitability measure for these loans, as inEquations (1)and (2). For the remaining loans, we can only observe the realized payments and the occurrence of default through the end of our sample period, so we estimate the expected profit using the probability of future default events, the likely recovery in case of default, and the projected future payments. Online Appendix A provides more details of the estimation. Throughout the paper, we report the results for the completed loans using the actual cash flows and for the ongoing loans using the expected profit. 3.2. Descriptive Statistics We describe loan, borrower, and vehicle characteristics for human- and machine-underwritten loans separately inTable 1. Using data on the number of loan applications provided by the lender, the average monthly funding rates of the machine and human are 4.61% and 4.68%, respectively. The human underwriters have a 1.5% higher funding rate (statistically insignificant), which shows that the human and machine are bidding on roughly the same number of applications. Our sample contains 70,033 loans funded by human underwriters and 70,990 loans funded by the machine. Table 1.Summary Statistics Table 1.Summary Statistics Notes.This table reports separate summary statistics for the human- and machine-underwritten loans between January 2013 and July 2019. The mean, standard deviation, and the difference in means are reported for borrower, vehicle, and loan characteristics, as well as loan outcomes. Standard errors are clustered by year-month and dealership. ***, **, and *Statistical significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. As for variables that reflect the selection criteria of human versus machine, borrowers in the human-underwritten loans, on average, have a higher credit score, a higher likelihood of homeownership, a lower likelihood of prior bankruptcy, and a higher DTI ratio. These differences reflect the differential approval criteria of the human and machine underwriters. Relative to humans, the machine sets an APR that is approximately 50 basis points higher and acquires loans with slightly larger discounts. But the clearest performance difference is in the loan outcomes: the default likelihood is 24.5% for human-underwritten loans, about 3 percentage points (p< 0.01) higher than for machine-underwritten loans. Overall, the summary statistics suggest that machine-underwritten loans have higher APRs ex ante and perform better ex post. The next section reports the variation in default rate, APR, and the number of loans funded by human and machine across deciles of loan characteristics. 3.3. Human and Machine Decision Criteria In this section, we shed light on the decision criteria of human and machine underwriters by comparing the default rate, APR, and number of loans originated across loan dimensions such as credit score, debt-to-income ratio, LTV, and loan amount. Figure A.7 in the Online Appendix divides the loans into deciles of credit score each month, then reports the average default rate and APR. Panel A of Figure A.7 in the Online Appendix shows that the machine- and human-underwritten loans have similar default rates for high-credit-score borrowers, with the human-underwritten loans even having slightly lower default rates in the top two credit score deciles. However, human-underwritten loans have significantly higher default rates for low-credit-score borrowers. In general, the slope of the relationship between credit score and default is flatter for the machine than for humans: a decile decrease in the borrower’s credit score translates into a default rate that is 2.2 percentage points higher for human underwriters but only 1.0 percentage points higher for machine underwriters. Similarly, Panel B shows that there is more heterogeneity in the APR charged by human underwriters across deciles of credit score, relative to the machine. The machine and human underwriters charge similar APRs for low-credit-score deciles, but the human underwriters charge significantly lower APRs for the high deciles. Moreover, despite the higher default rate, on average, for human-underwritten loans, the APR on these loans is generally lower across the credit score deciles than the APR for the machine-underwritten loans. These findings are consistent with human underwriters relying more on credit score in pricing loans, relative to machine underwriters. They also suggest that the machine is better at distinguishing between more risky and less risky borrowers. Similarly, in unreported results, we observe more heterogeneity in human-underwritten loans than machine-underwritten loans across other salient characteristics, such as debt-to-income and LTV ratios.
article.4. Machine- vs. Human-Underwritten Loan: 4.1. Loan-Level Profit In this section, we compare the profitability of loans underwritten by a machine algorithm and loans underwritten by humans. To highlight the difference in performance between the machine and humans, we focus on loan-level profit and do not consider the total profitability of the machine experiment. We observe neither the capital investment in the automation project nor the project’s impact on payroll. The identification comes from the random assignment of loans to either machine or human underwriters. In this randomized setting, any difference in loan outcome can be attributed to the difference between the respective underwriting processes of humans and the machine. Importantly, neither the borrowers nor the dealers are aware of the lender’s experiment, so there is no concern about self-selection of dealers or borrowers into certain types of loans. We run the following Ordinary Least Squares regression: Our main variable isMachine, an indicator that equals one if the deal is underwritten by the machine and zero if it is underwritten by a human. In the baseline estimates, we use no control variables other than origination year-month fixed effects because the applications are randomized and the observed borrower and loan characteristics reflect the choices by the machine or human. For completeness, we also report all the results with a full set of controls, including loan, borrower, and vehicle characteristics, in the Online Appendix. All regressions are clustered by year-month and dealership (two-way clustering). Table 2reports the results. Column (1) shows that the coefficient ofMachineis positive and economically significant (p< 0.01) for the uncensored loans. The machine generates 2.5 percentage points higher profit, which is a 9.5% improvement relative to the humans’ mean loan-level profitability of 26.4%. Column (2) ofTable 2reports the regression result for thecompletedloans, which include both uncensored loans and censored loans that either defaulted or were prepaid during our sample period. The results show that machine underwriting generates 2.75 percentage points higher profit on completed loans.20Furthermore, columns (3) and (4) show that, relative to humans, the machine generates 2.7 percentage points higher profit in both the censored loans and the full sample—differences that are statistically and economically significant. In both cases, this corresponds to a 10.2% improvement over the profitability of human-underwritten loans.21 Table 2.Difference in Loan Profit of Human- and Machine-Underwritten Loans Table 2.Difference in Loan Profit of Human- and Machine-Underwritten Loans Notes.This table reports estimates from panel regressions of loan profit on whether the loan is underwritten by the machine. The dependent variable is the loan-level profit ratio (profit/initial investment).Machineis an indicator equal to one if the loan is underwritten by the machine and zero if the loan is underwritten by a human. “Uncensored” loans are those whose original terms do not extend beyond the end of our sample period. “Completed” loans are those that have been completed during our sample period. “Censored” loans are those whose original terms extend beyond our sample period. Standard errors are clustered by year-month and dealership and are shown in parentheses below the coefficient estimates. ***, **, and *Statistical significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. We next examine whether the difference in profitability is absorbed in a linear regression that controls for loan, borrower, and vehicle characteristics. For example, suppose that loans to borrowers with lower DTI are more profitable and that the difference in loan performance is due to the machine’s selecting borrowers with lower DTI. If this is the case, then controlling for DTI should absorb part of the performance difference we observe. Table A.3 in the Online Appendix shows that the results hold even when we control for a full set of observable characteristics in our sample. These characteristics include LTV, loan amount, loan term, credit score, homeownership, income, DTI, whether the vehicle is used or new, and vehicle age, as well as year-month, day of the week, day of the month, dealer, make, and model fixed effects.22This finding suggests that the performance difference between machine and human is due to factors that extend beyond controlling for characteristics in a simple linear setting. We shed light on these underlying channels inSection 5. We next examine whether potential adjustments to loan terms by dealership finance officers affect our results. The dealership finance officers do not know whether the loans are underwritten by the machine or a human; therefore, it seems unlikely that they would treat the two loan types differently enough to drive the difference in performance we observe. Nevertheless, we examine this possibility by replicating our results for a subset of loans whose terms were not adjusted by dealership finance officers. The results, shown in Table A.4 in the Online Appendix, are statistically and economically strong and virtually identical to the results inTable 2. This finding confirms that our results are not driven by the dealerships treating human-underwritten loans and machine-underwritten loans differently. We further compare the difference in profitability at the underwriter level. Our sample contains 153 human underwriters. Figure A.9 in the Online Appendix shows the distribution of the median loan profitability of underwriters in our sample, including the machine. The median profitability varies from 28.6% to 38.3%. The machine profitability is 37.1%, which would fall at the 92nd percentile of the human underwriters during our sample period.23 Overall, our results suggest that the machine selects and prices loans that yield a significantly larger average profit than loans underwritten by humans.24These results are robust in different subsamples of the data and with a full set of control variables.25 4.2. Pricing The higher profitability of the machine-underwritten loans may be due to higher interest rates or mitigation of borrowers’ default risks. In this section, we compare the pricing of the loans underwritten by machines and humans. We estimate a regression of the APR onMachineand other controls: Similar to the results on profitability, we first estimate the regression with year-month fixed effects and without controls. Standard errors are clustered by year-month and dealership.Table 3shows that machine-underwritten loans have a significantly higher APR on average; in the uncensored sample, the machine prices the loans 46.2 basis points higher than the humans do. The machine-loan APRs are 44.1 and 44.2 basis points higher (p< 0.01 for both) for the censored loans and the full sample, respectively. The pricing difference also holds after controlling for observable loan characteristics (Table A.5 in the Online Appendix), suggesting that, on loans with similar characteristics, human underwriters offer a lower APR than the machine. Table 3.Difference in Interest Rates of Human- and Machine-Underwritten Loans Table 3.Difference in Interest Rates of Human- and Machine-Underwritten Loans Notes.This table reports estimates from panel regressions of interest rates on whether the loan is underwritten by the machine. The dependent variable is the contracted interest rate of a loan (APRpercent).Machineis an indicator equal to one if the loan is underwritten by the machine and zero if the loan is underwritten by a human. “Uncensored” loans are those whose original terms do not extend beyond the end of our sample period. “Completed” loans are those that have been completed during our sample period. “Censored” loans are those whose original terms extend beyond our sample period. Standard errors are clustered by year-month and dealership and are shown in parentheses below the coefficient estimates. ***, **, and *Statistical significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. In principle, this finding is consistent with human underwriters underpricing their loans to win the dealership auction. If the underwriters are doing this, one might expect them to underwrite more loans. Consistent withDeFusco et al. (2021), who show that loan take-up from lower prices is small in a consumer credit setting, we observe that the funding rate is 1.5% higher for human underwriters than for the machine. Moreover,Figure 1shows that human underwriters particularly underprice loans with high credit scores and that they originate considerably more of these loans than the machine does. The figure shows a strong negative correlation between the residual APRs and the number of loans funded by the machine versus human underwriters; human underwriters offer a lower risk-adjusted APR to high-credit-score borrowers, and their funding rate for those loans is considerably higher. The pricing by human underwriters might take into account unobservable characteristics and nonstandard information that the humans obtain from the dealers—factors we cannot control for in our regression. Such nonstandard information might help human underwriters to select a less risky set of loans with a lower ex post default rate. We next examine this possibility. 4.3. Default Rate Earlier we found that human underwriters offer lower APRs even after we control for observable characteristics. If the humans’ lending decisions are based on unobservable characteristics and nonstandard information that the dealers reveal to them, then their loans might be less risky than the machine-underwritten loans, which would explain the lower APRs for them.26If this is the case, then the lower risk should show up in the ex post default rate.Table 4compares the realized default rates between human- and machine-underwritten loans. Column (1) of Panel A shows that the machine-underwritten loans are 1.7 percentage pointslesslikely to default, which is equivalent to a 5.3% improvement (p< 0.05) over the default rate for humans. The machine is associated with a 7.2% lower (p< 0.001) default rate for censored loans and a 6.8% lower (p< 0.001) default rate for the overall sample. The dependent variables in columns (1)–(5) of Panel B are indicators equal to one if the loan defaults within 12 months, 18 months, 24 months, 30 months, and 36 months of the loan origination, respectively.Machineis an indicator equal to one if the loan is underwritten by the machine and zero if the loan is underwritten by a human. “Uncensored” loans are those whose original terms do not extend beyond the end of our sample period. “Completed” loans are those that have been completed during our sample period. “Censored” loans are those whose original terms extend beyond our sample period. We also compare theearlydefault rates of the machine- and human-underwritten loans. We followFuster et al. (2019)and look at actual early default rates within 12, 18, 24, 30, and 36 months of origination. We run the following regression: The results are presented in Panel B ofTable 4. The baseline default rates in the first 12 and 18 months are relatively small—3.3% and 6.9%, respectively—with no performance difference between machine- and human-underwritten loans. However, over the 24-, 30-, and 36-month horizons, the machine’s default rate is 6.9%, 8.0%, and 8.3% lower (p< 0.05 or lower for all three) than the human underwriters’ rate, respectively. Table A.6 in the Online Appendix shows that the differences in the default rate are even larger after controlling for the observed characteristics. The results show that an algorithmic underwriter is better at evaluating the default risk of subprime loans. This finding complementsFuster et al. (2019), who find that mortgages by FinTech lenders have lower default rates than mortgages by legacy lenders. In contrast to theFuster et al. (2019)setting, however, our randomized setting ensures that our results are primarily attributable to differences between the underwriting processes of the machine and human underwriters.
article.5. Examining the Sources of the Machine: Despite having been used for decades in many loan markets, human underwriting systems have limitations that could help explain their underperformance relative to the machine. (Recall that, in our setting, the human-underwritten loans are less profitable, have lower interest rates for the same risk profiles, and have higher ex post default rates overall.) For example, to keep the underwriting process simple, human underwriting systems typically tier prospective borrowers based on risk indicators such as LTV ratio and credit score. These discrete cutoffs can result in borrowers at different risk levels being grouped in the same tiers and thus offered similar interest rates, which in turn can lead to inefficient pricing (at best) and agency conflicts (at worst). Another limitation is a longer response time for application processing. A purported advantage of automated underwriting is that the response often takes just a few seconds, which could lead to less price competition for the machines’ submissions. In our setting, however, the lender delayed the response time of the machine by a few minutes so that it matched the human underwriters’ typical response time. In addition to preventing the underwriting experiment from being revealed, this would have eliminated any advantage related to response time.27 A third limitation may be the human underwriters’ limited capacity for analyzing complex data. In cases where the default prediction is complicated, human underwriters may find it more difficult to analyze the risk and price the loan, relative to the machine. Taking these limitations into account, we explore, in this section, the two most plausible explanations for our findings: (1) agency conflicts in underwriting and (2) capacity for analyzing complex data. 5.1. Machine Premium at Critical LTV Cutoff A possible explanation for why human underwriters offer lower risk-adjusted pricing (relative to the machine) involves conflicts of interest between the underwriters and the lender. By underpricing loans, human underwriters can maximize their chance of winning the loan auction and increase their monthly commissions.28The data provider does not release individual employee compensation details, but the subprime auto industry provides some guidance in this area—It reports that the average monthly salary of a loan officer is $4,400 (Tracey 2020) and that underwriters receive, on average, a $41 commission per contract and additional quarterly compensation for portfolio performance. If this channel is in play, we expect that the performance difference between human and machine underwriters will be more pronounced when the potential for conflict of interest is highest. To examine agency issues, we analyze the loans around a critical cutoff—an LTV of 125%—that our lender consistently uses as a guideline to categorize consumers into a high-risk tier.29A loan with an LTV just above 125% demands a higher APR than a loan with an LTV just below 125%.30Loans above the cutoff also face tighter restrictions on other risk measures (e.g., debt-to-income ratio). Therefore, human underwriters can offer more competitive prices and take more risks in other dimensions when loans are just below the 125% threshold. In our setting, this simple LTV guideline creates a potential for agency conflict. A human underwriter can improve her chances of winning loans—and maximize her monthly commission—by selectively approving the riskiest loans within a risk-pricing tier while offering those loans at the competitive APR of that tier. In contrast, loans with an LTV ratio just above or far below the cutoff (e.g., 115% or 120%) cannot be priced as competitively because they are not the riskiest in their tiers. They are also less likely to be selected by the dealership, as they limit its ability to extract financing profits. Because applications immediately below the 125% threshold are more likely to be accepted, they garner the most attention from underwriters. We use five approaches to test whether the performance difference between machine- and human-underwritten loans is more pronounced at LTV values just below 125% than at LTV values further below or just above the threshold. First, we examine the ratio of the human-underwritten to machine-underwritten loans around the LTV 125% cutoff. If human underwriters prefer loans just below the cutoff but the machine has no preference, we expect the ratio to increase just below the cutoff. Indeed,Figure 2shows that the loans just below the cutoff in our sample are 6.4 times more likely to be underwritten by humans than by the machine.31 Second, inTable 5, we compare the key characteristics of human- and machine-underwritten loans around the cutoff. The table shows that, just below the cutoff, loans funded by human underwriters have significantly riskier borrower profiles than loans funded by the machine. Here,LTV125is an indicator that equals one if the loan’s LTV is between 124.5 and 125. Relative to the machine-underwritten loans, the human-underwritten loans in this LTV range have a 0.21 standard deviation lower credit score and a 0.13 standard deviation higher DTI; that is, they are riskier in both dimensions. Moreover, the likelihood that a borrower has a prior bankruptcy is an additional 0.32 standard deviation higher for the human-underwritten loans than for the machine-underwritten loans in this LTV range. These differences are statistically significant (p< 0.01) and suggest riskier profiles for human-underwritten loans just below the cutoff. Table 5.Borrower Risk Factors Around LTV Ratio of 125% Table 5.Borrower Risk Factors Around LTV Ratio of 125% Notes.This table reports risk factors of borrowers around the loan-to-value (LTV) ratio of 125%. The sample includes loans with LTV ratios ranging from 122.5% to 127.5%. The dependent variables are the borrower’s standardized credit score (column 1), standardized debt-to-income ratio (column 2), and an indicator equal to one if the borrower has prior bankruptcy history (column 3).LTV125is an indicator equal to one if the loan’s LTV ratio is between 124.5% and 125% and zero otherwise.Machineis an indicator equal to one if the loan is underwritten by the machine and zero if the loan is underwritten by a human. Standard errors are clustered by year-month and dealership and are shown in parentheses below the coefficient estimates. ***, **, and *Statistical significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. Third, we compare the default rates for machine- and human-underwritten loans around the cutoff. Panel A of Table A.7 in the Online Appendix shows that, for loans with an LTV just below 125%, human-underwritten loans have an 8-percentage-point higher default rate in the first 36 months relative to machine-underwritten loans.32This represents a 38.1% lower default rate for the machine-underwritten loans just below the cutoff. This difference is considerably larger than the 8.3% lower default rate for the machine in the full sample. Fourth, in Panel B of Table A.7 in the Online Appendix, we examine the difference in loan pricing around the cutoff. To adjust the APR for the risk, the setting controls for loan, borrower, and vehicle characteristics. We find that, just below the cutoff, human underwriters generally offer lower prices (i.e., APR) and lower risk-adjusted prices. Below the LTV of 125%, within the LTV bandwidth of 124 to 126, machines underwrite loans with an APR that is an incremental 27.4 basis points higher than the human loans. Columns (2) and (3) show that the results hold in wider bandwidths around the cutoff. These findings suggest that, just below the cutoff, human underwriters do not compensate for their loans’ higher risk by charging a higher APR. Finally, inTable 6, we compare the profitability of machine- and human-underwritten loans around the cutoff. The results in column (1) show that the machine premium increases to 27.9% for loans just below the cutoff (a 7.5-percentage-point increase, relative to the 26.9% profitability for human underwriters). Column (2) shows that the results do not change when we include fixed effects (dealer, car make, car model) and additional controls for loan characteristics (loan-to-value, loan amount, loan term, discount, debt-to-income), borrower characteristics (credit score, homeownership, prior bankruptcy), and vehicle characteristics (new or used, age). Table 6.Loan Profit Around LTV Ratio of 125% Table 6.Loan Profit Around LTV Ratio of 125% Notes.This table reports the effect of machine underwriting on loan profit around the LTV threshold of 125%. The sample includes loans with loan-to-value (LTV) ratios ranging from 122.5% to 127.5%.LTV125is an indicator equal to one if the loan’s LTV ratio is between 124.5% and 125% and zero otherwise.Machineis an indicator equal to one if the loan is underwritten by the machine and zero if the loan is underwritten by a human. The dependent variable is the loan-level profit ratio (profit/initial investment). The regression in column (2) controls for loan characteristics (loan-to-value, loan amount, term, discount), borrower characteristics (credit score, homeownership, prior bankruptcy, debt-to-income ratio), and vehicle characteristics (new or used, age). Standard errors are clustered by year-month and dealership and are shown in parentheses below the coefficient estimates. ***, **, and *Statistical significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. In addition to the explanation described above, we examine two alternative explanations for the patterns we observe below the 125% LTV cutoff. The first is that simplifying the underwriting decisions based on discrete cutoffs exposes lenders to the winner’s curse phenomenon, in which the winning bid in an auction exceeds the true value of the discounted cash flows. The discontinuous increase in the price of riskabovethe cutoff could make human underwriters more likely to win loansbelowthe cutoff—even if they do not submit more bids in that range.33In contrast to human underwriters, whose bids just below the cutoff are more competitive and thus more likely to be chosen by the dealer, the machine fits its predictive models to a continuous variable space, so it is not susceptible to the same issue. Thus, under the winner’s curse scenario, the observed pattern may occur because the human underwriters win more auctions just below the cutoff and not because they submit more bids. To examine this possibility, we use a sample of loan applications that underwriters approved and submitted to the loan auction but failed to win (i.e., the bid was not accepted by the dealer). Figure A.11 in the Online Appendix shows that, just below the cutoff, the number of failed bids by human underwriters, like the number of successful bids, is five times higher than for the machine. That is, humans are much more likely to approve and bid on loans just below the cutoff than just above it, relative to the machine. Therefore, although the discrete classifications in the underwriting guidelines could expose lenders to the winner’s curse problem, the observed patterns appear to be primarily attributable to the human underwriters’ aggressive bidding on loans below the 125% LTV cutoff. The second alternative explanation for the patterns around the cutoff is that dealers inflate their automobiles’ book values to keep the LTV below 125%, and that the machine is better at identifying this pattern.34We examine this possibility using residual book values from a hedonic model based on vehicles’ new/used indicator, age, remaining warranty years, and mileage, as well as the vehicle’s make and model fixed effects. Panel A of Table A.8 in the Online Appendix reports the hedonic regression model. TheR2of the model is 82.04%, with 71.2% coming from within the fixed effect groups. The strongest explanatory variables are the new/used indicator, vehicle age, and mileage. In our sample, new cars are $4,221.2 more expensive than used ones. Each additional year reduces the car’s value by $892.8, and each additional mile reduces the car’s value by $0.055. We use the residuals from this model to test whether the percentage deviations from the estimated hedonic prices are different below and above the cutoff for human and machine. Panel B of Table A.8 in the Online Appendix shows that, after controlling for loan and borrower characteristics, deviations from the hedonic model just below the cutoff are statistically indistinguishable from those at other LTV values. Moreover, the book values are no more inflated for human-underwritten loans just below the cutoff than for machine-underwritten loans (the interaction term is not statistically significant). These results suggest that book values are no different below or above the 125% LTV cutoff, so the patterns observed around the cutoff are not driven by the dealerships. Intuitively, this finding makes sense, as it seems unlikely that the 4,000-plus car dealerships in our sample would systematically tailor their applications to the rules of specific lenders.35 Our results show that loans that are originated below the LTV cutoff are consistently less profitable when they are underwritten by humans. InFigure 3, to reexamine whether human underwriters are systematically approving loans that machines would not underwrite, we group loans with LTVs between 124.5% and 125% into buckets based on their ex ante default likelihood (from the credit model in Online Appendix A). The results show that human underwriters fund loans under the cutoff that have close to zero or negative returns on average, and the average negative performance of these loans can be predicted ex ante based on the historical loan performance available to the lender. This finding suggests that the human-underwritten loans in this LTV range not only garner lower average loan-level profits but may even have a higher marginal cost than marginal revenue.36 Overall, the results in this section suggest that human underwriters, unlike machine underwriters, are more likely to originate risky loans immediately below the critical LTV cutoff without a commensurate increase in price. Importantly, the difference between the machine’s and the human underwriters’ performance in terms of profitability and defaults increases significantly just below the cutoff, consistent with potential agency conflicts between the human underwriters and the lender.37The findings are also consistent with the notion that a machine underwriting process can help mitigate such agency conflicts. 5.2. Machine Premium for Complex Loans Kahneman (1973)describes how humans’ limited attention and cognitive resources hinder the information processing surrounding complex problems. In our context, the underwriters’ primary role is to predict the default risk of the loan applications and price the loan accordingly. In more complicated cases, human underwriters may find it more difficult to analyze the risk and price the loan. Therefore, the performance gap between the machine and humans might increase with loan complexity. Conversely, one argument against automated underwriting is that manual underwriting is supposedly better at handling complex borrowers, as human underwriters (unlike machines) can bring discretion and subjective judgment to these cases (Lipshitz and Shulimovitz 2007). Consistent with humans using subjective judgment, the human-underwritten loans in our sample show variations in APR that are too large to be explained by observable risk indicators. Table A.9 in the Online Appendix reports that, after we control for all hard information, there is more variation in the residual price of human-issued loans than in the residual price of machine-issued loans. Moreover, observable risk indicators yield a lowerR2in explaining APRs for human-underwritten loans. Such discretion by humans, whether due to underwriter experience, heuristics, or additional nonstandard information, may be especially important in evaluations of low-credit-score borrowers (Iyer et al. 2016). Borrowers with poor credit records and low incomes experience larger income volatility than higher-income customers (Dynan et al. 2012). Moreover, a lack of insurance or savings (or other, less visible factors) can make them more vulnerable to unexpected shocks. As a result, predicting their likelihood of default is likely to be more complicated. Consequently, we analyze the performance of machine and human underwriters for two types of complex cases: borrowers with low income and credit scores, and loans we identify as complicated using a comprehensive measure of loan complexity. 5.2.1. Borrowers with Low Credit Score and High Debt-to-Income Ratio. To assess whether the performance gap between the machine and humans increases with loan complexity, we start by comparing the loan profitability of machine underwriting and human underwriting along two dimensions: credit score and DTI ratio.Figure 4plots the difference in the performance of machine-underwritten loans and human-underwritten loans across quantiles of credit score and DTI. The difference in profitability is close to zero for high-credit-score, low-DTI loans. However, the profitability of machine-underwritten loans is remarkably higher—reaching 6 percentage points—for loans to low-credit-score, high-DTI borrowers.Figure 4shows that the difference in profitability changes almost monotonically across credit score and DTI.38Figure A.12 in the Online Appendix shows, importantly, that this pattern is mainly driven by variation in the performance of human underwriters across these dimensions. Panel A of Figure A.12 in the Online Appendix illustrates the machine-underwritten loans’ relatively stable profitability across borrowers with different credit scores and the human-underwritten loans’ underperformance in low-credit borrowers. Panel B shows the results for debt-to-income ratio. We examine these patterns more formally inTable 7. We first construct two indicators using credit score and DTI:Low Credit Score, which is equal to one if the debtor’s credit score is below the median credit score in the sample; andHigh DTI, which is equal to one if the debtor’s DTI is above the median. We also capture the interaction of credit score and DTI using the variableLow Credit Score-High DTI, which equals one if both variables are equal to one. We estimate a regression of loan profit onMachineand these constructed variables. The dependent variable is the loan-level profit ratio (profit/initial investment).Machineis an indicator equal to one if the loan is underwritten by the machine and zero if the loan is underwritten by a human.Low Credit Scoreis an indicator equal to one if the debtor’s credit score is below the median credit score in the data and zero otherwise.High DTIis an indicator equal to one if the debtor’s DTI is above the median DTI and zero otherwise.Low Credit Score-High DTIis an indicator equal to one ifLow Credit ScoreandHigh DTIare both equal to one, and zero otherwise. Standard errors are clustered by year-month and dealership and are shown in parentheses below the coefficient estimates. ***, **, and *Statistical significance at the 1%, 5%, and 10% levels, respectively. Table 7shows that the machine significantly outperforms in all specifications as a baseline. In addition, column (1) shows that the interaction ofMachineandLow Credit Scorehas a positive coefficient (p< 0.01). For a low-credit-score loan, the machine outperforms humans by 13.1%, which is 3.4 percentage points39higher than the baseline human-underwriting profit of 25.9%. Similarly, column (2) shows that the machine’s profit premium over humans is 12.7% for the high-DTI loans. Finally, in column (3), we look at the interaction of credit score and DTI. The machine premium for low-credit-score, high-DTI borrowers is 16.9%—a 4.4-percentage-point increase relative to the baseline of 25.9% for human underwriters.40Panel A of Table A.11 in the Online Appendix shows that this result holds within the subsamples of uncensored, censored, and completed loans, and Panel B of Table A.11 in the Online Appendix shows that the performance difference increases monotonically across quartiles of credit score and DTI. These results are consistent with machine underwriters outperforming human underwriters mainly when it is more difficult to predict the loan outcome. 5.2.2. Complexity Measure. To further assess whether the performance gap between the machine and humans increases with loan complexity, we capitalize on a complexity measure. Using the intuition developed in the previous section, we construct a measure that captures the difficulty of predicting default based on different loan characteristics. First, using more than 15 years of historical data from before the beginning of the automation experiment, we calculate the forecast errors both for the credit model developed in Online Appendix A and for a linear model (which we use as a robustness check).41We define the forecast error as the absolute value of the difference between the predicted default likelihood and the actual outcome of the loan. We then estimate a regression of the magnitude of the forecast errors on loan, borrower, and vehicle characteristics using the historical uncensored data from January 1997 to December 2012.42Finally, we use the out-of-samplefitted valuesfrom this regression to estimate the difficulty of predicting default conditional on characteristics for the loans in the experiment starting in January 2013. We call this the complexity measure. Given that default is a binary variable, the magnitude of the forecast errors for predicting default is a function of the default likelihood.43This leads to a correlation between the default likelihood and the expected complexity measure that we calculate conditional on loan characteristics. (Figure A.13 in the Online Appendix plots this relationship.) Therefore, to distinguish the effect of complexity from that of default likelihood, we directly control for default likelihood in our regression specifications. Table 8examines how the difference in profitability of machine- and human-underwritten loans changes depending on loan complexity, controlling for changes in default likelihood. The interaction term in column (1) shows that, for a one-standard-deviation increase in complexity, the machine-underwritten loans generate an additional 80 bps higher profit. Column (2) shows that the results hold when we control for loan and borrower characteristics and year-month, dealer, make, and model fixed effects. Columns (3) and (4) reveal that the results are virtually the same when we use the forecast errors from a linear model to predict default. Notably,Figure 5shows that these results are driven by variation in the profitability of human-underwritten loans across the complexity dimension. Specifically, the human-underwritten loans yield a lower profitability for more complex loans, whereas machine-underwritten loans yield relatively stable profits across dimensions. We next compare the number of loans that humans and the machine fund across deciles of complexity. Figure A.14 in the Online Appendix shows that human underwriters are less likely to fund complex loans, especially those in the extreme complexity deciles. This may be because human underwriters cannot effectively process these loans and less complicated ones and thus cannot produce the same level of profitability. The dependent variable is the loan-level profit ratio (profit/initial investment).Machineis an indicator equal to one if the loan is underwritten by the machine and zero if the loan is underwritten by a human.Default LLis the loan’s default likelihood. Finally, we examine the extent to which loan complexity accounts for the dollar value of the profit difference. Again dividing the loans into complexity deciles, we calculate the aggregate dollar profit difference between the machine- and human-underwritten loans. Figure A.15 in the Online Appendix reports the dollar value in each decile as a percentage of total additional dollar profit generated by the machine. The highest complexity decile accounts for 36% of the profit difference, and the second highest accounts for 23%. Thus, the top three complexity deciles account for approximately 75% of the total additional dollar profit generated by the machine. Overall, these results indicate that loan complexity is the main contributor of the performance difference between human- and machine-underwritten loans. 5.3. Discussion Fair loan pricing relies critically on the assumption that the variables used for assessing the creditworthiness of prospective borrowers are not a proxy for membership in a protected class. In addition to the larger question of how FinTech affects lending in general, there is significant interest in how automation might affect discriminatory lending practices (Dobbie et al. 2021). Will algorithmic underwriting in indirect lending eliminate potential bias arising from human discretion at the lender, or will it simply replace taste-based discrimination with statistical discrimination (Morse and Pence 2020)? If auto dealership finance staff still interact directly with consumers, will anything prevent them from marking up loans that had been fairly priced? Given the importance of auto credit within society, these questions merit additional examination. Here, we examine whether the algorithm exhibits bias toward communities with different racial compositions. Although our data does not allow us to identify the borrower’s race, we can discern each community’s racial makeup by connecting the dealership’s zip code with zip-code-level demographics from the 2015 American Community Survey (ACS), which shows the population of workers across different races. We merge each loan’s data with the demographic information from the survey, then test whether the machine underwriters and human underwriters have different funding rates and profits across communities of different racial compositions. Table A.13 in the Online Appendix shows that algorithmic underwriting does not exhibit a significant bias in funding rates for racial minorities. In Panel A, no statistical bias against communities with a larger percentage of Black or Asian workers is evident. If anything, machine underwriting exhibits a smallpositivebias toward communities with a larger composition of Hispanic workers. However, this effect is economically small and statistically significant only at the 10% level, and it disappears after we control for loan, borrower, and vehicle characteristics in Panel B. These findings are consistent with the interpretation that the machine does not exhibit any racial composition–based bias in its funding rate. Overall, we find no meaningful evidence of discrimination in funding rates by either the machine or the human underwriters. The machine’s statistically weak bias in favor of the Hispanic communities is explained by differences in loan characteristics and traditional risk measures (e.g., credit score). We next examine whether the effects of algorithmic underwriting on loan profitability vary with the racial composition of the community. Table A.14 in the Online Appendix shows that the profitability of machine-underwritten loans is not statistically different in communities with a larger share of Black, Asian, or Hispanic workers. These results hold both without controls (Panel A) and with controls for loan, borrower, and vehicle characteristics (Panel B). Overall, these findings suggest that in this setting, there is scant evidence that automation results in statistical discrimination. We find that racial composition of a community does not appear to impact funding rates or loan profitability when machine-underwriting is introduced.
article.6. Conclusion: The recent trend toward replacing human underwriters—the traditional gatekeepers of consumer finance—with machine underwriters raises important questions about how the automation of financial decisions is likely to impact businesses’ efficiency and profitability. In this paper, we examine the effect of the automation of auto loan underwriting and contrast it with the traditional human-based underwriting practice. Our setting—an experiment in which loan applications are randomly assigned to machine or human underwriters—allows us to study the automation process while keeping demand-side factors constant (in terms of both observables and unobservables). The results show that an algorithmic underwriting system outperforms a human underwriting system, resulting in higher loan profits and lower default rates. The machine performance is stable across various risk dimensions and loan characteristics, whereas the human performance deteriorates for riskier and more complex loans. Moreover, the performance difference is more pronounced just below an underwriting threshold with a high potential for agency conflict. Overall, our results suggest that delegating financial decisions to machines is particularly important in complex situations and when the potential for agency conflicts is high. These findings contribute to the literature on the role of technology in finance by quantifying the disparate performance of human and machine underwriters and showing how automation can mitigate agency issues in underwriting. Although the extant literature discusses the impact of FinTech and machine learning algorithms, it does not clarify whether the observed impact is attributable to improved algorithms, differences in customer selection, or the replacement of humans with machines. In this paper, we directly study the impact of fully replacing human underwriters with machines in a consumer lending market and examine mechanisms that give rise to the differential outcomes. One strength of our setting is the absence of soft information. Such information varies significantly across underwriting settings, making it difficult for many studies to generalize their results. The contribution that our setting yields is critical and timely, as consumer and business lenders are increasingly switching from human-based underwriting systems to full automation (Chappell et al. 2018). The significant advantages that accrue to the machine-based system will likely give rise to new competitive dynamics. Although this paper addresses some of automation’s consequences for lenders, important questions remain. For example, how will the lenders’ transition to algorithms affect borrowers’ access to capital markets? What are the longer-term consequences of automation, especially in booms and recessions? More broadly, does machine underwriting enhance societal welfare? Higher loan costs are borne by borrowers and could push some of the subprime borrowers into financial distress. Even if the borrowers are able to avoid a loan default and vehicle repossession, higher auto loan payments could still lead them to default on other financial obligations (e.g., credit cards). Although this study sheds light on the incentives to automate the lending process, further research is warranted on the related questions.
